text: Which of the following statements about module import declarations (JEP 511) are correct? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: import module java.base; imports all public packages exported by the java.base module.
Module imports are transitive — importing a module also imports all modules it requires.
A module import declaration can appear in any source file, including unnamed-module code.
Module import declarations replace the need for a module-info.java file.
Module imports are a compile-time convenience and do not affect the module graph at runtime.

explanation: import module java.base; brings all exported packages of the module into scope for that file. It works in unnamed module code too. It does not affect the runtime module graph and does not make dependencies transitive. It does not replace module-info.java.

text: Which of the following is a valid compact source file that can be run directly with java HelloWorld.java?

options.text: A file with a public class containing a static void main(String[] args) method.
A file with no class declaration and a top-level void main() statement.
A file where the first class declared is implicitly the main class and has an instance main() or static main() method.
Any .java file regardless of structure.
A file with a module-info.java declaration in the same directory.

explanation: Compact source files (JEP 512) allow a .java file where the first class is implicitly the main class. It can use an instance main() method (JEP 495) or the traditional static main(). No explicit class declaration is required when using unnamed classes.
source: JEP 512 – Compact Source Files and Instance Main Methods (Java 25): https://openjdk.org/jeps/512 | JEP 495 – Simple Source Files and Instance Main Methods (preview): https://openjdk.org/jeps/495

text: Which of the following are true about flexible constructor bodies (JEP 513)? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: Statements can appear before the super() or this() call as long as they do not access the instance being constructed.
super() can now be placed anywhere in the constructor body, not just the first statement.
Flexible constructor bodies allow argument validation and transformation before the superclass constructor runs.
Fields of the subclass can be read before the super() call.
The feature removes the requirement to call super() entirely.

explanation: Flexible constructor bodies allow statements before super()/this() that do not reference the uninitialized instance (no this.field access). This is useful for validating or transforming arguments before passing them to super(). super() must still be called and cannot be read before it.

text: Which of the following are true about Scoped Values (JEP 487, now final in Java 25)? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: A ScopedValue is immutable once bound within a scope.
ScopedValue is a replacement for ThreadLocal in all use cases.
A ScopedValue is accessible to all threads spawned in its scope via StructuredTaskScope.
ScopedValue.where(key, value).run(() -> ...) binds the value only for the duration of the lambda.
ScopedValue values are mutable and can be updated by child threads.

explanation: ScopedValues are immutable within their bound scope. They propagate automatically to child threads created via StructuredTaskScope. The binding is scoped to the run()/call() block. They do not replace all ThreadLocal use cases (e.g., mutable per-thread state) and child threads cannot mutate them.
source: JEP 487 – Scoped Values (Fourth Preview) → finalized in Java 25 as JEP 509: https://openjdk.org/jeps/487 | https://openjdk.org/jeps/509

text: What does the following code print?

options.text: The code does not compile.

explanation: Gatherers.windowFixed(3) groups stream elements into non-overlapping windows of exactly 3. Stream of 6 elements produces two windows: [1,2,3] and [4,5,6]. The result is a List<List<Integer>>.
source: JEP 485 – Stream Gatherers (finalized in Java 25): https://openjdk.org/jeps/485

text: Which of the following Gatherers are available in the standard java.util.stream.Gatherers class? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: Gatherers.windowFixed(int size)
Gatherers.windowSliding(int size)
Gatherers.scan(supplier, integrator)
Gatherers.batch(int size)
Gatherers.fold(supplier, integrator)
Gatherers.mapConcurrent(int maxConcurrency, function)

explanation: The standard Gatherers class (JEP 485) provides: windowFixed, windowSliding, scan, fold, and mapConcurrent. There is no Gatherers.batch() — use windowFixed for fixed-size batching.
source: JEP 485 – Stream Gatherers (finalized in Java 25): https://openjdk.org/jeps/485 | Java SE 25 API Docs: https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/25/docs/api/java.base/java/util/stream/Gatherers.html

text: Which command correctly runs a multi-file source-code program where Main.java depends on Helper.java, both in the same directory?

options.text: java Main.java Helper.java
java --source 25 Main.java
java Main.java
javac Main.java Helper.java && java Main
java *.java

explanation: With multi-file source-code programs (JEP 512), running java Main.java automatically compiles and resolves other source files referenced by Main.java in the same directory. Only the entry-point file is specified on the command line.
source: JEP 512 – Compact Source Files and Instance Main Methods (Java 25): https://openjdk.org/jeps/512
